text: The Tree of Life is the most pretentious thing ever committed to film. waterfalls and whispering and dinosaurs, please. Malick mistakes vagueness for profundity and you all eat it up because admitting you were bored feels uncultured.
label: subjective_take

text: the documentary fiction blur in Close-Up and Memories of Murder has me wanting to explore proper documentaries that feel like thrillers. I usually skip docs but these changed my mind. recommend the most cinematic gripping documentaries you know.
label: curation

text: Lost in Translation is just two rich attractive people being sad in a nice hotel and we're supposed to find it profound. Coppola's whole vibe is melancholy as a luxury good. nothing actually happens and I'm meant to call it subtle. pass.
label: subjective_take

text: rewatched Stalker AGAIN sorry I'm obsessed, but it made me want to dig into the Strugatsky brothers and Soviet sci fi literature that got adapted. less a watch rec really, more, what are the best book to film adaptations in art cinema worth seeking out.
label: curation

text: everyone's favorite A24 horror is just slow burn nothing with a loud noise at the end. the elevated horror label is marketing. these films mistake quiet for tension and call dread what is actually just me waiting for something to happen.
label: subjective_take

text: Babylon is three hours of Chazelle screaming LOOK HOW MUCH I LOVE MOVIES in your face. the maximalism is just noise, the elephant, the snakes, it's a coke binge mistaking excess for passion. I needed a nap and an aspirin.
label: subjective_take

text: Roma shooting in 65mm black and white with these slow lateral pans treats Cleo's domestic labor with the same epic scale usually reserved for war films. Cuaron's camera literally won't stop moving past her, the framing insists she's the protagonist of history.
label: analysis

text: bruh the single take hallway fight in Oldboy isn't just a flex. shooting it flat side-on like a 2D beat em up removes any editing safety net, so Oh Dae-su's exhaustion reads in real time. you can SEE him slow down because there's no cut to hide it.
label: analysis

text: Dune part two is just empty spectacle honestly. all that mise-en-scène and not a single frame made me feel anything. Villeneuve is a cold technician with nothing to say, it's gorgeous wallpaper for people who want to feel smart. mid.
label: subjective_take

text: the bridge of Inception cross cuts four dream levels at different time scales so an edit in the van takes minutes in the snow fort. Nolan uses parallel editing where each cut also jumps you between speeds of time. the montage rhythm IS the premise.
label: analysis

text: honestly after Oppenheimer I went down a rabbit hole on films about scientists and moral compromise and realized there's a whole genre here. anyway long story short, what are the best films about people who build something terrible and then have to live with it.
label: curation

text: I watched Dancer in the Dark and haven't fully recovered, the way the musical fantasy breaks against the brutal reality. von Trier is cruel but I can't look away. should I keep going into his filmography and if so in what order, where do I start.
label: curation

text: Cinema Paradiso is shameless emotional manipulation and the famous kissing montage ending is designed to make you cry without earning it. nostalgia bait of the highest order. I cried and then felt cheap about it, which is its own kind of bad.
label: subjective_take

text: 2001 is a beautiful screensaver and nothing more. all the people talking about the dialectic of man and technology are reading a thesis into a movie that's just slow shots of space. impressive for its time, unwatchable now.
label: subjective_take

text: Past Lives left me staring at the ceiling thinking about every version of my life I didn't live, the whole in-yun concept. devastating in the quietest way. I want more films about roads not taken and the people we let go. recommendations please.
label: curation

text: the way Aftersun and The Florida Project both put a child's limited perspective at the center to make the adult tragedy hit sideways really gets me. I love films that filter heavy things through a kid's eyes. what else does this well.
label: curation

text: Anatomy of a Fall replays the argument as audio only over the courtroom, then shows the reconstruction. Triet uses the gap between what we hear and what gets visualized to make the point that we're all just rebuilding a truth we never actually witnessed.
label: analysis

text: I just finished Stalker and I'm sort of wrecked by it, the way it treats faith and desire as the same impossible thing has been rattling around my head all day, and it made me realize I've barely scratched Soviet sci fi. what else from that scene should I be watching, ideally more philosophical than action.
label: curation

text: honestly Drive uses the elevator scene to compress romance and brutality into one continuous space, the light goes soft gold for the kiss then hard fluorescent for the stomp. Refn changes the color temperature mid scene to split the Driver in two.
label: analysis

text: Bergman shoots Persona with these merging face compositions where Alma and Elisabet's profiles literally fuse into one frame. it's not subtle but the point is the dissolve of identity, and he's doing it in-camera with lighting and blocking, not just dialogue.
label: analysis

text: ok after Mad Max Fury Road I finally appreciate that action can be high art when the geography is this clear and the editing this precise. I want more action films that are actually well crafted not just loud. what are the genuinely great action movies.
label: curation

text: The Shining's Steadicam follows Danny's tricycle at kid height so the hotel feels enormous and the cuts to the dead twins arrive at his eyeline. Kubrick literally lowers the camera to make us small. the geography of the Overlook never quite matches up either, intentional.
label: analysis

text: lowkey the jump cuts in Breathless were partly to cut runtime down but Godard kept them because they make Michel feel weightless and consequence free. the broken continuity IS the character, a guy skipping through life with no transitions or accountability.
label: analysis

text: Nomadland is poverty tourism with a beautiful sunset filter. Zhao aestheticizes precarity and calls it humanism. McDormand frowning at vistas for two hours. it wants me to feel something true and it's all just pretty sad imagery, no bite.
label: subjective_take

text: I think Synecdoche New York is either the most ambitious failure or success I can't decide, but it made me want more films that try to contain an entire human life. swing for the fences stuff. recommend me the most audaciously ambitious films, hits or misses.
label: curation

text: honestly Moonlight's three chapter structure following one person across a life has me wanting more films built as triptychs or anthologies of a single soul. that formal elegance really moved me. what other films use structure this beautifully.
label: curation

text: the Room scene in Stalker, Tarkovsky finally gives them what they want and they refuse to enter. the camera just holds on the threshold in this long static take and the rain starts falling inside. the duration forces you to sit in their failure of faith.
label: analysis

text: Killers of the Flower Moon did not need to be three and a half hours. Scorsese fell in love with the material and forgot to edit. yes the subject matters, that doesn't make the bloated runtime a virtue. I was exhausted not moved.
label: subjective_take

text: Whiplash edits the drumming scenes on the actual hits, cutting on the snare and cymbal so the rhythm of the montage matches the rhythm of the music. by the finale the cuts get faster than is comfortable, which puts you inside Andrew's panic.
label: analysis

text: I think the bonfire scene in Portrait of a Lady on Fire where the dress catches is the single most beautiful image I've seen this decade, and now I'm chasing films with that painterly composed quality, every frame a painting. recommend the most visually composed films.
label: curation

text: the dolly zoom on the staircase in Vertigo, pushing the camera back while zooming in, makes the space stretch sickeningly. it's not a gimmick, it externalizes Scottie's acrophobia so we feel the vertigo instead of just being told about it.
label: analysis

text: ok unpopular but Apocalypse Now is overlong and messy and the back half completely falls apart. yeah the production was insane, that's not the same as the film being coherent. Brando mumbling in the dark is not the profundity you think it is.
label: subjective_take

text: Mad Max Fury Road centers the action on the frame's middle axis on every cut so your eye never has to hunt. that's why you can follow chaos at 200 cuts a minute. Miller storyboarded for eyeline continuity, it's editing engineering not luck.
label: analysis

text: Moonlight reshoots the same beach across three chapters with three actors and slightly different color grades, cooler then warmer. Jenkins uses the consistent location with a shifting palette to show Chiron is one person being repainted by who's allowed to love him.
label: analysis

text: rewatching Eternal Sunshine for the tenth time and crying again over the way it argues that you'd choose the pain anyway. I'm a sucker for films about memory and whether love is worth the heartbreak. give me more in this exact emotional wheelhouse.
label: curation

text: Tarkovsky films are a test of endurance not an artistic experience. the so called meditative pacing is just a man in love with his own slowness. I respect the craft I guess but Mirror felt like a punishment I didn't earn.
label: subjective_take

text: honestly the climax of Hereditary pins Toni Collette against the ceiling in the corner of the frame, and Aster uses these dollhouse wide shots all film so the family looks like dolls being arranged. the framing tells you they were never free agents from shot one.
label: analysis

text: honestly after watching a bunch of Haneke I'm both disturbed and hooked, the cold clinical cruelty that forces you to examine your own spectatorship. it's a lot. should I keep going and which ones are essential, and is there anyone with a similarly unsparing gaze I should know.
label: curation

text: Burning never confirms whether the cat or the well or the murder are real, and Lee Chang-dong shoots Jongsu's discoveries with the same flat naturalism as everything else. by refusing a stylistic tell for fantasy vs fact the form keeps you in his class resentment paranoia.
label: analysis

text: honestly after Anatomy of a Fall and A Separation I'm realizing I love films that are basically a marriage falling apart treated with forensic precision. no villains, just two people and the wreckage. recommend me the best divorce and separation dramas.
label: curation

text: the long take dance finale in Beau Travail unlocked something, I now realize I love films that culminate in a release of pure movement after holding everything back. what other films build to a cathartic physical or musical explosion like that.
label: curation

text: Yi Yi shoots people through windows and reflections constantly so the city is always layered over their faces. Edward Yang frames every character behind glass to show how modern Taipei mediates and isolates them even in intimacy. the reflections are the theme.
label: analysis

text: Tarkovsky uses the switch from sepia to color in Stalker only once they enter the Zone. the ordinary world is drained and dead, the supposedly dangerous Zone is lush and saturated. the color grade inverts where we think safety lives.
label: analysis

text: rewatched The Godfather and what strikes me now is how it's really about a family business swallowing a son whole, the slow corruption of Michael. I'm in the mood for more sprawling crime sagas with that operatic tragic arc. what should I watch.
label: curation

text: rewatched There Will Be Blood and the opening fifteen wordless minutes are just staggering, character built entirely through labor and that screeching score. I love films that trust images over exposition. recommend me more films that say a lot with no dialogue.
label: curation

text: Memories of Murder ends with Bong having the detective stare directly into the lens, breaking the fourth wall to implicate the audience and the unsolved real case. the look is an accusation, the form reaches out of the unresolved fiction at you.
label: analysis

text: honestly horror as a genre peaked and everything now is either jump scare garbage or A24 slow nothing pretending to be about trauma. the trauma metaphor thing is so played out. just scare me, stop making me write an essay about grief.
label: subjective_take

text: I cannot get into French New Wave at all. Godard especially feels like a film student who read too much theory and forgot to entertain. the jump cuts and the cool detachment just read as smug to me, sorry to the cinephiles.
label: subjective_take

text: honestly Pulp Fiction has aged terribly. the dialogue everyone worships is just Tarantino showing off how many movies he watched. it's smug and hollow and the nonlinear structure is a gimmick to hide that there's no there there.
label: subjective_take

text: Drive is a perfume ad masquerading as a film. the synthwave, the satin jacket, the long meaningful silences, it's all surface cool. Refn has incredible taste and nothing to say. style as a personality, I was bored under the neon.
label: subjective_take

text: the new All Quiet on the Western Front is just war is bad for two hours with a nice color palette. it has nothing the genre hasn't said better. people moved by it haven't seen enough war films, the anti war message is baby's first thesis.
label: subjective_take

text: Casablanca is fine. there I said it. it's a perfectly competent old romance that got mythologized. the dialogue is quotable sure but the whole thing feels stiff and stagey now, I don't understand the all time great status.
label: subjective_take

text: The Whale is emotional blackmail dressed as compassion. the fat suit, the relentless suffering, Aronofsky again confusing pain for depth. it wants my tears and refuses to earn them. manipulative and frankly kind of gross.
label: subjective_take

text: ngl I avoided silent films forever thinking they'd be boring and then Sunrise completely changed my mind, the visual storytelling is more sophisticated than half of what's out now. where do I go next, what are the essential silents for a recent convert.
label: curation

text: Tokyo Story shoots almost everything from the low tatami mat height, the famous Ozu pillow shots, and never moves the camera. the static low angle treats the family with patient respect, then the kids keep leaving the frame and you feel the abandonment spatially.
label: analysis

text: ngl the way Villeneuve frames Paul against the empty desert in Dune part two, always tiny in the corner of these massive 1.43 imax compositions, lowkey tells you he's being swallowed by the prophecy before the script ever says it out loud. the scale of the framing IS the warning.
label: analysis

text: the new Zone of Interest approach to atrocity, keeping it all offscreen on the soundtrack, completely changed how I think horror should work. restraint hits harder than gore. what other films convey horror through what they refuse to actually show.
label: curation

text: honestly after Uncle Boonmee I'm realizing slow contemplative Thai and Southeast Asian cinema is a huge gap for me and Apichatpong is incredible. where do I even go from here, what are the essential films from that region I'm missing entirely.
label: curation

text: ngl Parasite uses verticality as the entire visual thesis. the Parks live up the stairs, the Kims descend into the flood. every time someone moves between classes Bong stages it as climbing or falling down steps. the blocking is the class system.
label: analysis

text: Saltburn is the dumbest movie I've seen in years and the bathtub scene is just shock for shock's sake. Fennell thinks transgression equals insight. all aesthetic provocation, zero actual class critique despite what the film thinks of itself.
label: subjective_take

text: Nope was a mess honestly. Peele had three movies fighting inside one and the spectacle critique theme got buried. everyone defending it as layered is just doing his homework for him. I left the theater shrugging.
label: subjective_take

text: bruh Zone of Interest puts all the camp horror on the soundtrack, the screams and shots are just ambient behind the garden party, and Glazer hid cameras to shoot it flat and surveillance like. the banality is formal, the atrocity is literally offscreen and you supply it.
label: analysis

text: the way Anatomy of a Fall sits in ambiguity about whether she did it, never letting you off the hook, has me wanting more courtroom or trial films that refuse easy answers. what are the great morally ambiguous trial movies?
label: curation

text: honestly after Memories of Murder I'm fascinated by films based on real unsolved crimes, the way the lack of resolution becomes the theme. Zodiac too. recommend me the best films about obsession with a case that's never cracked.
label: curation

text: rewatched In the Mood for Love and I think I finally get the WKW thing, the longing, the repetition, the way the camera lingers on what people can't say. now I'm hungry for more films about restraint and unspoken love. recommendations for quiet devastating romances?
label: curation

text: I genuinely cannot stand Greta Gerwig films. everyone calls Lady Bird this perfect coming of age but it just felt like quirky white girl problems with a good soundtrack. wildly overpraised, the auteur worship is out of control.
label: subjective_take

text: I find Paul Thomas Anderson exhausting. The Master especially, all that gorgeous 70mm and these scenes that just sit there refusing to resolve. people call it enigmatic, I call it withholding. it kept me at arm's length on purpose.
label: subjective_take

text: Punch Drunk Love scores Barry's anxiety with this percussive industrial clatter that only resolves into Shelley Duvall's He Needs Me when he's with Lena. PTA uses the soundtrack's dissonance and release as a literal map of his mental state.

text: honestly the diner scene in Heat works because Mann finally puts De Niro and Pacino in the same frame after keeping them apart the whole film with shot reverse shot from a distance. the eventual two shot lands precisely because he withheld it.

text: the way Saul fia keeps everything but Saul's face out of focus to dramatize psychological survival in a death camp is one of the boldest formal choices I've seen. I want more films with a radical formal restriction like that. what else commits this hard.
label: curation

text: Burning has been living in my head for weeks, that ambiguity about whether anything we saw was real, the class resentment simmering under everything. I love films that refuse to confirm what happened. recommend me your best ambiguous unreliable narrator films.
label: curation

text: rewatched Goodfellas and that Copacabana oner still makes me grin, the way the unbroken shot seduces you into the lifestyle. I love when form and theme lock together like that. recommend films where a signature technique actually means something.
label: curation

text: Oppenheimer is overrated and I'm tired of pretending the editing is some genius juxtaposition. it's three hours of men in rooms talking and everyone calls it a masterpiece because Nolan. emperor has no clothes type beat.
label: subjective_take

text: ok but like why is Dune considered good. it's so slow and serious and everyone whispers their lines about spice. I wanted to like it. the worldbuilding everyone gushes about just felt like homework. maybe I'm dumb idk.
label: subjective_take

text: I went into The Lighthouse expecting nonsense and got genuinely unsettled by the slow descent into madness in an enclosed space. now I want more claustrophobic two hander films about people unraveling together. recommendations?
label: curation

text: honestly Beau Travail builds the whole film toward Galoup's solo dance to Rhythm of the Night, and Denis cuts all the military drills like choreography first so the explosion of movement at the end reads as everything repressed finally bursting. the editing trains you for it.

text: Tar is two and a half hours of a woman talking and I refuse to believe anyone found it gripping. people called the long Juilliard scene a tour de force, it's a lecture. a film that loves the sound of its own intelligence.

text: Blade Runner 2049 is gorgeous and utterly soulless. all that production design and atmosphere in service of nothing. it mistakes slow for deep and pretty for meaningful. a hollow object I admired and forgot instantly.

text: Phantom Thread scores Reynolds' obsessive routines with this swooning Greenwood piano that swells then sours when Alma disrupts him. the music tells you the power balance shifting before the dialogue does, the romance and the sickness share one melodic line.

text: the way All That Jazz cuts dying against showbiz really sold me on the autobiographical filmmaker self portrait as a genre, 8 and a half too. I love directors turning the camera on themselves. recommend me the great films where a filmmaker dissects their own life.

text: Interstellar is an emotional masterpiece and easily Nolan's best, the Zimmer organ alone makes me sob every time. people who nitpick the science are missing the point entirely, this movie is about love across time and it's perfect.

text: the new Star Wars films are corporate slurry and even the prequels had more of a vision, at least Lucas was swinging at something. Disney made nostalgia delivery devices with no soul. I feel nothing watching any of them.
label: subjective_take

text: honestly Bergman bums me out and not in a productive way. all that anguished closeup staring into the void about God's silence, it's just a depressed Swede monologuing for two hours. I respect it from a distance and never want to watch it again.
label: subjective_take

text: Tarkovsky has completely ruined faster movies for me, after Mirror and Stalker I just want films that trust silence and duration and let an image breathe for once. what are the essential slow cinema films I need to see, and please don't just say Bela Tarr I know.

text: Lynch passing has me revisiting everything and Mulholland Drive still feels like a live wire, the way dread just leaks out of ordinary rooms. I want more of that uncanny everyday horror feeling. films where reality feels slightly wrong but you can't say why?

text: the unbroken oner through the Copacabana in Goodfellas isn't showing off, it's showing us why Karen falls for the life. the continuous glide past every door that opens for Henry makes the seduction of power physical. cut it up and the spell breaks.

text: the red coat in Schindler's List is obvious but the smarter choice is shooting the whole thing handheld documentary style except the liquidation, which Spielberg shoots cleaner. the formal restraint there makes the horror feel observed not staged.

text: okay so I finally watched 2001 properly, no phone, big screen, and the match cut alone justified the whole thing. now I'm itching for more big swing sci fi that's actually about ideas not lasers. what are the thinking person's sci fi essentials I'm missing.

text: Marriage Story is just two attractive people we're told are sad, and the famous fight scene is actors doing ACTING at each other for an Oscar reel. Baumbach wants applause for an honesty he hasn't actually shown me. theatrical and hollow.

text: I think Playtime might be the funniest film where I barely laughed out loud, Tati's whole comedy of architecture and tiny background gags. it rewired my sense of humor. what other comedies reward you for watching the whole frame like that.

text: lowkey the way A Separation shoots arguments through doorways and stairwell railings keeps putting bars and frames between the couple. Farhadi never gives you a clean two shot of them together once the divorce starts, the architecture pre-divides them.

text: La La Land is a hollow nostalgia object for people who've never actually watched the musicals it's referencing. Chazelle's homage is skin deep, the so called bittersweet ending is cheap, it just wants you to feel things it didn't earn.
label: subjective_take

text: I think Yi Yi might be one of the most humane films I've ever seen, the way it gives every family member their own full inner life across three hours. now I want more of these generous sprawling family epics. what else captures a whole life like that.

text: Get Out is a good thriller that got wildly overrated by people who wanted it to be more than it is. solid premise, neat ending, but the discourse made it sound like Citizen Kane. it's a fun horror movie, calm down everybody.
label: subjective_take

text: honestly Drive made me realize I love films where the style IS the substance, where mood and music do most of the talking. even if the plot is thin I'm here for the vibe. recommend me the most atmospheric style forward films, neon optional.

text: The Lighthouse is two guys yelling in black and white for two hours and everyone pretends the symbolism is deep. it's vibes and farts. Eggers is all surface texture and the mythological mise-en-scène is a smokescreen for having no actual point.
label: subjective_take

text: Barry Lyndon was shot with NASA lenses to film candlelit interiors with no electric light. but the point isn't the tech flex, the natural light flattens these scenes into living paintings, which freezes Barry inside a class he can climb but never belong to.

text: rewatched Apocalypse Now and despite the bloat the boat going upriver as a descent into madness still works on me. I love the journey into darkness structure. recommend films built around a trip that's really a psychological descent.
label: curation

text: Synecdoche New York is Kaufman disappearing up his own concept. the recursive theatre within theatre is clever for ten minutes then it's just a sad man's diary written in metaphors. ambitious sure, but pretentious and emotionally suffocating.
label: subjective_take

text: Wong Kar-wai shoots Chungking Express with smeared step printed motion around the still cops so the world blurs while they stand frozen in loneliness. the frame rate trick separates them from time, isolation rendered as a difference in motion blur.
label: analysis

text: ok the practical in camera effects in Eternal Sunshine, walls disappearing mid scene, have me craving more films that do their magic without CGI. the tactile handmade quality hits different. recommend films with great practical or analog visual tricks.
label: curation

text: The thing about Jaws is Spielberg breaks the 180 degree line on purpose during the beach attack to disorient you spatially. you lose track of where the shark is relative to the swimmers, which is the whole terror. controlled geography breakdown.

text: Aftersun cuts between the camcorder footage and the film footage so you're never sure which memories are recorded and which are reconstructed. Wells uses the medium itself, lo-fi DV vs film grain, to dramatize how a daughter rebuilds a father she barely understood.

text: the way Burning and Parasite both use physical space, up and down, inside and outside, to map class really fascinates me. Korean cinema does class so spatially. recommend me films that turn architecture and space into class commentary.

text: I think Kubrick is a cold misanthrope and his films prove it. all that perfect symmetrical composition and zero warmth. you admire his work the way you admire a machine. Barry Lyndon is gorgeous and I felt absolutely nothing.

text: Boyhood is a gimmick. filmed over twelve years okay cool, but if you remove that fact it's a completely unremarkable family drama where nothing happens. Linklater got a pass for the concept alone and I never bought it.
label: subjective_take

text: been on a huge film noir kick lately, rewatched Double Indemnity and Out of the Past back to back and the fatalism is just chef's kiss, the way these guys narrate their own doom. anyway I've done all the obvious ones, hit me with the deep cut noirs nobody talks about.
label: curation

text: everything Wong Kar-wai does looks the same to me, slow motion, neon, longing, gorgeous people not talking. it's a mood board not a movie. In the Mood for Love is two hours of yearning aesthetics with no actual story to hang it on.
label: subjective_take

text:
This film(s) really is something I'd put off seeing for some time. I was never a Lars von Trier fan (could completely feel his inventive brilliance in Antichrist and Melancholia, but neither film left anything in me, almost entirely forgetting them other than the feeling of unsettledness they gave). I honestly didn't want to watch 5 hours of f&cking as some sort of cinematic experiment, but a recent review on TrueFilm and comments there convinced me somewhat spontaneously to order the BluRay. The film wasn't what I had previsioned. First of all, vol 1 is really a beautiful, enaging film, and though it is somewhat about "addiction" (in this case sex addiction, but could standing in for all), its a much more human film that really felt like it was also about all the things that lead to addictive "release" or shelter in pleasure, which gave it both an incredible specificity (this character, this person), but also a universal humanity. Not just in addicts, but in all of us, in how we use and relate to any pleasure. The conversation that drives the retelling of this woman's life is just beautifully shot in a spare, "monk like" room and serves as an expertly tempo'd weave into and out of the stories of a developing psychology. But, afoot in all of this are some very profound ideas that go well beyond simply the story of a psychology. Once we get to the theme of the Angler we start to feel that there are some VERY big philosophical stakes...at stake, bringing pressure onto the very foundations of our sense of morality, Good vs Evil, passing through Christianity and probably back to Plato. Seligman becomes a very interesting compound figure in this...and reference to Plato's use of the example of the "angler" in the dialogue The Sophist, can be no accident. (The "angler" is evocative of the sophist - not a seeker of truth, but somewhat of an intellectual conman, which can map onto later Christianity's "deceiver" Devil mythos - who fishes for the youth, and teaches them with sophistry/falseness.) As Seligman describes how the older, bigger fish seclude themselves in the well protected nooks of a river, very hard to catch in solitude in solitude, we realize at some point he is really describing himself. And the fishing fly, the false bait, may very well be "Joe", who may become over time a very, very subtle seductress, daring him to "bite"...a "hook-er". He also may stand in for an intellectualizing film critic (who doesn't really feel a film directly), a theologically absolving confession priest, or a theorizing therapist (who doesn't actually "hear" his patient), or as I chose to see him eventually, something of Wim Wenders-like angel, who understands human beings at a remove. He is a completely asexual, intellect-oriented being, listening to a overly sexualized person, who is trying to convince him of her (evil?) sinful, awful character. The film reads at some level as almost a At The Gates of Heaven weighing of a soul, as Joe's life story unfolds, and the way von Trier braids all these levels together, from Plato to the grittiest part of human experience is nothing short of spectacular. Volume 1 kind of blew me away.

Volume 2 was a different story. I was really looking forward to it - in part because it had Dafoe and Goth, two of my favorite actors - but it veered in a much less satisfying direction. It's his film, and his vision, so I don't wish he did anything differently, but the spell of the first volume was broken. A large measure of this is because he took on various tropes of extreme sexuality (porn tropes like 2 black guys on a white girl, sado-masochism, gangbang), along with political tropes of debates over sexuality (an absolute brave but incredibly hard to watch abortion scene, meant to take on the bodily "reality" beneath these debates), that made the film much, much less unified. Perhaps some of this because part of the problem with "porn" is that it breaks the cinematic spell. As the philosopher Zizek once said (paraphrasing) "when you see porn you suddenly feel that the film exists just to show "this", film becomes a prop holding it up". This was not the effect IN the film, when taking on cliches and tropes of porn, in that the scenes felt very un-erotic, as least for me, they often felt clinical, as if dissecting the human sexual condition. But the breaking of the cinematic spell, in borrowing from porn, or casting scenes full of cliche, did happen (for me). This distancing, which may very well have been von Trier's philosophical goal. He's trying confront taboo, to strip down the human condition, but taboo also structures our eyes and how narraative is processed, so it made it also feel UNREAL (which I suspect was not his aim). By the time the film got to Joe joining the "other army" (in this case it felt like Dafoe was the Devil's stand in) and getting some sort of incoherent "collection" job, the story itself felt like it was falling apart for me. I had little narrative investment in her somehow moving to the Devil's side (if only allegorically, or metaphorically) and exacting a provocative "revenge" or flipping the script on men. Not only was the story not believable, the performances really were not as well (despite Gainsbourg being off the charts good elsewise). Even Dafoe and Goth were uncomfortably off-the-mark, and I felt like I had really entered into a film only of Ideas. Trying on ideas. Making points. Maybe some of this was intentional. It's possible, but the transgressive, graphic sex set-ups and aesthetics, the unrealistic story turns just made the film MUCH less enjoyable and interesting to watch (though the political, psychological, philosophical debates between Seligman and Joe remained strong).

The ending was absolutely fantastic. Yes, indeed, she was a "lure" of a kind, seducing the fallen or in-between angel, perhaps very much against her own will, positioning the dangers of sexuality put onto women in society, something the film suggests may even be connected to how the Intellect & the Body is divided by socialized gender. She was either an accidental, or very very subtle...or ideologically imposed "whore of Babylon" who could seduce even the most asexual being, and she perfectly gets her revenge, remembering to rack the pistol.

In the end the film is one that I'm very glad that I watched (thank you to True Film commenters who lead to me do so. I'd probably put it in the same category as Noe's Irreversible which was an incredible masterpiece, but maybe not a film I'd watch again. This film...I "might" watch again.
label: analysis

text:
So I ran into an article about the film Obsession and the headline immediately grabbed my attention.

“ Obsession is the GET OUT for White people”

I was taken aback by this because I just saw Obsession and not one time did Get Out cross my mind. Yes there's the scene at the table where Nikki repeatedly says "NO NO NO" like the famous line in Get out but outside of that I don't see the comparison.

Yet this author made it a point to basically say Obsession is a complete rip off. I don't know if she's being disingenuous or she's not familiar with the cautionary tale of the monkeys paw or Aladdin. Basically “ be careful what you wish for”. But am I alone in thinking this is a horrible take on Obsession?

Btw, even though the author of this article made it about race let's not make sweeping generalizations here. I would like a mature discourse on her take especially considering I'm Black myself and enjoyed the hell out of Obsession.
label: subjective_take

text: I watched Persona and I'm not sure I understood it but I felt it, that dissolving of two identities into one. Bergman is a lot but I think I'm ready. where should I go next in his filmography, what are the essential ones to build up to.

text:
This movie kept me up all night. I should have known this would happen because when have I ever slept peacefully after a Haneke film?

I don't have more new thoughts to add to this film that haven't already surfaced in this subreddit and elsewhere except that it feels meta that this film lives on in cultural conversations as an "erotic film" or "that BDSM film". Heavy on the rot in erotic, I say. I've even seen Secretary (2002) recommended as a companion film. Personally I'd recommend Elle (2016) or Blue Velvet (1986). As I saw it, the film ends in a rape which is the final nail in the coffin of Erika's safely guarded inner world of desires, shame, control. Sure, initially the film lulls you into thinking this might be an erotic film, but it ends in psychosexual trauma. Categorically, or so I thought.

This cultural flattening of Erika's trauma and complexity into "that BDSM movie" reflects how I imagine Walter went on with his life too. Blithely, unaware of the damage he caused; perhaps this affair comes up between chuckles on a boozy night with friends about that weird scene he once had with his uptight piano teacher. I didn't see him as a character to have enough reflection to construe his actions as rape because every time he faces something uncomfortable be it his idea of Erika versus Erika herself, or the puking incident, he responds to it with rage and domination.

Is this memory of the film as a dark erotic film a reflection of something about us? About how we treat victims of sexual assault particularly those who do not come across as sympathetic? From the conversations about the film I scanned, admittedly during my sleepless feverish night, it disturbed me to see not everyone concludes Walter's actions are rape. He even taunts her with "is this what you wanted?", in what I read as an admission of his awareness that this is not what she wanted. He knows and she knows. It feels that much like Walter who dismissed this woman with the weird sexual desires, culturally too the film lives on in memory as that weird kinky transgressive film and not a despairingly traumatic one.
label: analysis

text: been deep in Italian neorealism after Bicycle Thieves gutted me, the way it finds tragedy in something as small as a stolen bike. I've done the famous ones. what are the lesser known neorealist films worth tracking down.
label: curation

text: honestly I think most 'slow cinema' is just directors who can't edit calling it a style. Bela Tarr, Tsai Ming-liang, hold a shot of a wall for ten minutes and call it contemplative. emperor's new clothes for people who want to feel patient and deep.
label: subjective_take

text: the long static dinner in Festen, lit only by available light with that nervy DV camera, Vinterberg's Dogme rules strip out any cinematic cushioning so the abuse reveal lands like it's happening at your own table. the formal poverty is the brutal honesty.
label: analysis

text: First Reformed shrinks to the boxy academy ratio and locks the camera into these symmetrical static frames so Toller is boxed in by his own rigid faith. Schrader's transcendental style withholds camera movement so the one levitation scene detonates.
label: analysis

text: y'all the bowling ball POV shot in The Big Lebowski gliding into the ball return is the Dude's dream logic literalized. the Coens use these floating camera moves only in dream sequences so the form tells you when we've left reality without a cut to black.
label: analysis

text: There's a reason In the Mood for Love uses slow motion in the noodle stall corridor. the step printing stretches these mundane passings into longing, and the repeated Yumeji theme cues it. WKW is editorializing time itself to show two people orbiting and never touching.
label: analysis

text: lowkey the aspect ratio shifts in The Grand Budapest Hotel actually mean something. Anderson uses 1.37 for the 1930s, 2.35 for the 60s, 1.85 for the present. the frame shape tells you what era you're in before any title card. format as chronology.
label: analysis

text: Aftersun was so boring I almost walked out. yeah yeah the camcorder, the grief, I get it. it's a feeling stretched into ninety minutes with no story. people sobbing over it are sobbing over their own dads not this film.
label: subjective_take

text: ok the Safdies have me in a chokehold after Uncut Gems, that relentless anxiety, the feeling of a panic attack in film form. I kind of love being stressed out by a movie. recommend me the most nerve shredding anxiety inducing films you know.
label: curation

text: There Will Be Blood opens with like 15 minutes of no dialogue, just Daniel digging and breaking his leg and dragging himself. Anderson establishes the whole character through physical labor and the Greenwood score's dissonant strings before a single word.
label: analysis

text: I went in skeptical on Portrait of a Lady on Fire and left absolutely floored by the way it uses the gaze, who looks and who is looked at. I want to keep pulling that thread. recommend me films that are really about looking, about the act of seeing someone.
label: curation

text: rewatched Chungking Express and the lovesick cop eating expired pineapple is somehow the most relatable thing ever filmed. WKW gets loneliness in the city. I want more films about urban isolation and chance encounters. what should I add.
label: curation

text: lowkey Uncut Gems mixes the dialogue so everyone talks over everyone with no clean channel, and the Lopatin synth score never lets up. the Safdies engineer auditory anxiety, you can't find a quiet pocket to breathe, which is exactly Howard's life.
label: analysis

text: after Festen and the whole Dogme thing I'm weirdly into the idea of films that thrive under strict self imposed rules. the constraint makes them raw. what other films or movements built something great out of formal limitation, point me somewhere.
label: curation

text: Andrei Rublev shifts to color only in the final icon sequence after three hours of black and white suffering. Tarkovsky withholds color so the art at the end feels like the only justification for all the brutality. form as theodicy basically.
label: analysis

text: Dancer in the Dark switches from handheld digital grime to bright multi camera musical sequences whenever Selma dissolves into fantasy. von Trier uses the abrupt format clash so the numbers feel like dissociation, beauty as a survival mechanism from a grim reality.

text: Close-Up has Kiarostami restage real events with the actual people who lived them, then ends on a long lens shot where the audio keeps cutting out 'accidentally'. the form admits cinema can't fully capture truth even while it's chasing it. doc and fiction fused.

text: ok hot take but Hitchcock is wildly overrated. the auteur theory crowd canonized him and now we can't say his films are creaky and dated. Vertigo is a slog, Psycho is a one trick. fight me.

text: honestly Cleo from 5 to 7 unfolding almost in real time as a woman waits for test results really moved me, the ticking clock of mortality. I love films that play with real time. recommend me the best films that happen in something close to real time.
label: curation

text: okay the lighting in The Godfather. Gordon Willis keeps Brando's eyes in shadow under the brow the entire opening so you literally cannot read him. they called him the prince of darkness for it. the underexposure is a character choice not a mistake.

text: The Conversation ends with Harry tearing his apartment apart looking for a bug and the camera does this slow surveillance pan over the wreckage. Coppola turns the watcher into the watched formally, the camera adopts the gaze Harry fears. paranoia made structural.
label: analysis

text: Children of Men's long takes ruined other action scenes for me, the immersion when the camera won't cut is unmatched. I want to chase that feeling. recommend me films famous for their oner long takes that actually serve the story.
